lines.greek: δαιτρὸς δὲ κρειῶν πίνακας παρέθηκεν ἀείρας
παντοίων, παρὰ δέ σφι τίθει χρύσεια κύπελλα·
κῆρυξ δʼ αὐτοῖσιν θάμʼ ἐπῴχετο οἰνοχοεύων.
ἐς δʼ ἦλθον μνηστῆρες ἀγήνορες. οἱ μὲν ἔπειτα
ἑξείης ἕζοντο κατὰ κλισμούς τε θρόνους τε,
τοῖσι δὲ κήρυκες μὲν ὕδωρ ἐπὶ χεῖρας ἔχευαν,
σῖτον δὲ δμῳαὶ παρενήνεον ἐν κανέοισιν,
κοῦροι δὲ κρητῆρας ἐπεστέψαντο ποτοῖο.
οἱ δʼ ἐπʼ ὀνείαθʼ ἑτοῖμα προκείμενα χεῖρας ἴαλλον.
αὐτὰρ ἐπεὶ πόσιος καὶ ἐδητύος ἐξ ἔρον ἕντο
μνηστῆρες, τοῖσιν μὲν ἐνὶ φρεσὶν ἄλλα μεμήλει,
μολπή τʼ ὀρχηστύς τε· τὰ γάρ τʼ ἀναθήματα δαιτός·
κῆρυξ δʼ ἐν χερσὶν κίθαριν περικαλλέα θῆκεν
Φημίῳ, ὅς ῥʼ ἤειδε παρὰ μνηστῆρσιν ἀνάγκῃ.
ἦ τοι ὁ φορμίζων ἀνεβάλλετο καλὸν ἀείδειν.
αὐτὰρ Τηλέμαχος προσέφη γλαυκῶπιν Ἀθήνην,
ἄγχι σχὼν κεφαλήν, ἵνα μὴ πευθοίαθʼ οἱ ἄλλοι·
ξεῖνε φίλʼ, ἦ καί μοι νεμεσήσεαι ὅττι κεν εἴπω;
τούτοισιν μὲν ταῦτα μέλει, κίθαρις καὶ ἀοιδή,
ῥεῖʼ, ἐπεὶ ἀλλότριον βίοτον νήποινον ἔδουσιν,

literal: And a carver lifted and set before them platters of meats
of every kind, and placed golden goblets beside them;
and a herald went to and fro among them, pouring the wine.
Then in came the proud wooers, and thereafter
they sat themselves down in rows on couches and chairs,
and heralds poured water over their hands,
and maidservants heaped bread beside them in baskets,
and youths filled the mixing-bowls brim full of drink.
So they put forth their hands to the good cheer lying ready before them.
But when they had put from them the desire of food and drink,
the wooers, then other things were a care to their hearts,
song and dance, for these are the crowning glories of a feast.
And a herald placed a most beautiful lyre in the hands
of Phemius, who sang among the wooers under constraint.
He struck the strings and struck up a fair song.
But Telemachus spoke to bright-eyed Athena,
holding his head close, so that the others might not hear:
'Dear stranger, will you be angry with me for what I shall say?
These men care for such things, the lyre and song,
and easily, since they devour another's livelihood without atonement,
translations.elizabethan:
A carver raised and set before them platters
of every meat, and golden cups beside;
and still a herald came and poured the wine.
Then in the proud suitors came, and one by one
sat down in order on the seats and chairs.
Heralds poured water on their hands; the maids
heaped bread in baskets by them; and the youths
brimmed all the mixing-bowls; and every hand
went out to the good cheer that lay before them.
But when the suitors had put by desire
of meat and drink, their hearts were turned to else,
to song and dancing, crowns of any feast.
A herald set the lovely lyre in the hands
of Phemius, who sang because he must.
He struck the strings and raised a lovely song.
Then to bright-eyed Athena, leaning close
that none might overhear, Telemachus:
"Dear guest, wilt thou be wroth at what I say?
These men have care for nothing but the lyre
and singing, easily, for they consume
another's living and pay nothing back."
translations.modernist:
A carver lifted platters of meat of every kind and set them down, and put gold cups beside them. A herald kept coming with the wine.

Then the proud suitors came in. They sat down in rows on the chairs and the seats. Heralds poured water over their hands. Maids heaped bread in baskets. Boys filled the bowls to the brim. The men reached out for the food in front of them.

When they had eaten and drunk all they wanted, they thought about other things. Song and dancing. That is what finishes a feast. A herald put the fine lyre into the hands of Phemius. He sang for the suitors because he had to. He touched the strings and began a good song.

Telemachus leaned his head close to bright-eyed Athena so the others would not hear.

"Dear stranger. Will you be angry if I say something? These men care about the lyre and the singing. It comes easy to them. They are eating another man's property and they pay nothing for it."
translations.storybook:
A carver brought in great platters piled with every kind of meat, and set golden cups beside them, and a herald kept going round and round pouring out the wine.

Then in came the greedy suitors! They sat themselves down in long rows on the chairs and benches. Servants poured water over their hands, and maids piled up baskets of bread, and boys filled the wine bowls right to the brim. And every one of them reached out and helped himself.

When they had eaten and drunk just as much as they wanted, they began to want other things: songs and dancing, which is how a feast always ends. So a herald put a lovely lyre into the hands of a singer named Phemius, who had to sing for them whether he wanted to or not. He touched the strings and began a beautiful song.

Then Telemachus leaned his head very close to bright-eyed Athena, so that nobody else could hear.

"Dear guest," he whispered, "will you be cross with me if I tell you something? These men care about nothing but music and singing. And why should they not? It costs them nothing at all. They are eating up another man's food, and they never pay a penny for it."

murray.text: sat them down in rows on chairs and high seats. Heralds poured water over their hands, and maid-servants heaped by them bread in baskets, and youths filled the bowls brim full of drink; and they put forth their hands to the good cheer lying ready before them.

Now after the wooers had put from them the desire of food and drink, their hearts turned to other things, to song and to dance; for these things are the crown of a feast. And a herald put the beautiful lyre in the hands of Phemius, who sang perforce among the wooers;

and he struck the chords in prelude to his sweet lay. But Telemachus spoke to flashing-eyed Athena, holding his head close, that the others might not hear: “Dear stranger, wilt thou be wroth with me for the word that I shall say? These men care for things like these, the lyre and song,

full easily, seeing that without atonement they devour the livelihood of another, of a man whose white bones, it may be, rot in the rain as they lie upon the mainland, or the wave rolls them in the sea. Were they to see him returned to Ithaca, they would all pray to be swifter of foot,
